metadata.summary: A/B testing runtime with deterministic hashing, exposure/conversion tracking, statistical analysis, and SRM detection for reliable experimentation.

content.title: A/B Testing Implementation
content.summary: A/B testing runtime implementation covering deterministic variant assignment, exposure and conversion event tracking, statistical analysis (z-test for proportions, Wilson CI), and Sample Ratio Mismatch detection. Core rule: assignment must use deterministic hashing (hash(user_id || experiment_id) mod 100) — never random.choice — to ensure sticky bucketing across sessions, devices, and processes.

why.p: Non-deterministic assignment produces different variants for the same user on repeat visits, contaminating results. Tracking conversions without exposures removes the denominator, making results meaningless. Peeking at p-values before the precomputed end date inflates false-positive rate from 5% to 20–30%. SRM (skewed traffic split) invalidates all results and is almost always caused by bucketing bugs or redirect asymmetries.

list.item: Experiment design is complete (hypothesis, primary metric, MDE, sample size) and runtime is needed.
Multi-platform consistency: same user must get same variant on web, iOS, app, and email.
Building an event pipeline joining exposure → conversion in a stats engine (Snowflake, ClickHouse).
Wiring a feature flag into a real experiment with stratified randomization.
Traffic too low to reach statistical power (under ~1k weekly users on the surface) — use qualitative methods.
Changes affecting every user equally and irreversibly (DB schema migrations).
Cosmetic tweaks where test cost exceeds shipping cost.
Multi-armed scenarios with strong network effects (marketplace pricing) — A/B is biased; use switchback or geo splits.
Compliance-bound flows (KYC, payments) where variant differences create audit problems.

section.name: deterministic-variant-assignment
section.p: Hash-based variant assignment ensuring sticky bucketing across sessions, devices, and processes. Determinism Rule: Use hash(user_id || experiment_id) mod 100 for variant assignment. Never use random.choice() or any non-deterministic method. Non-deterministic assignment gives the same user different variants on repeat page loads, corrupting results. Deterministic hashing ensures the same user always receives the same variant, across processes, servers, and sessions.

rule.statement: Use `hash(user_id || experiment_id) mod 100` for variant assignment. Never use `random.choice()` or any non-deterministic method.
rule.rationale: Non-deterministic assignment gives the same user different variants on repeat page loads, corrupting results. Deterministic hashing ensures the same user always receives the same variant, across processes, servers, and sessions.

rule.statement: Salt each experiment with a unique experiment ID in the hash input. Successive experiments must not share buckets.
rule.rationale: Without per-experiment salting, users in variant A of experiment 1 will always be in variant A of experiment 2. This correlation biases results in experiments run on overlapping populations.

example.title: Deterministic assignment with SHA256 hashing
example.code:
import hashlib
def assign_variant(user_id: str, experiment_id: str, weights: dict[str, float]) -> str: """Deterministic assignment. weights = {"control": 0.5, "treatment": 0.5}""" hash_input = f"{user_id}:{experiment_id}".encode() hash_value = int(hashlib.sha256(hash_input).hexdigest(), 16) % 100
cumulative = 0 for variant, weight in weights.items(): cumulative += weight * 100 if hash_value < cumulative: return variant return list(weights.keys())[-1] # fallback to last variant
example.note: SHA256 ensures uniform distribution across the 100 buckets; the deterministic hash survives process restarts and server boundaries.

section.name: sample-ratio-mismatch
section.p: SRM detection and prevention. Sample Ratio Mismatch (SRM) occurs when observed exposure counts skew significantly from expected weights (e.g., 51/49 instead of 50/50), which almost always indicates a bucketing bug, redirect asymmetry, or bot filtering that hits one variant more. All results from an SRM experiment are invalid.

rule.statement: Run a daily SRM check: chi-square test on observed exposure counts versus expected weights. Pause the experiment if `p < 0.001`.
rule.rationale: SRM (observed 51/49 instead of 50/50) almost always indicates a bucketing bug, redirect asymmetry, or bot filtering that hits one variant more. All results from an SRM experiment are invalid.

section.note: SRM detection must happen before any p-value computation on the primary metric. Do not trust results from an SRM experiment under any circumstances.

section.name: exposure-and-conversion-tracking
section.p: ExperimentTracker with exposure deduplication and conversion event logging. Tracking Rules: Log an exposure event every time a user is shown the variant. Deduplicate on (experiment_id, user_id) within a session or day to avoid inflating denominators. Without exposure logging, the readout only shows "users who converted while assigned to X" with no denominator. Conversion rate = conversions / exposures; without exposures, you have no rate.

rule.statement: Log an exposure event every time a user is shown the variant. Deduplicate on `(experiment_id, user_id)` within a session or day to avoid inflating denominators.
rule.rationale: Without exposure logging, the readout only shows "users who converted while assigned to X" with no denominator. Conversion rate = conversions / exposures; without exposures, you have no rate.

rule.statement: Track exposures and conversions as separate events. Never derive exposures from conversion events.
rule.rationale: Deriving exposures from conversions creates survivorship bias — users who did not convert are invisible. This inflates all conversion rates and produces false significance.

example.title: ExperimentTracker Implementation
example.note: Exposures deduped within session prevents inflating denominators; conversions logged separately allow any user to convert regardless of exposure dedup window.

section.note: Log exposure on every render, dedupe on (exp_id, user_id, day). Without exposure log you cannot filter to "actually saw it".

section.name: statistical-analysis
section.p: Z-test for proportions, Wilson confidence interval, power calculation, and sample size formula. Test Selection Rule: Use a two-proportion z-test (or chi-square) for binary conversion metrics. Use a Welch t-test only for continuous metrics (revenue, time-on-page). Never use a t-test for binary outcome data. A t-test on binary (0/1) data violates the normality assumption for small samples and produces inflated false-positive rates. The z-test for proportions is exact for binomial outcomes.

rule.statement: Use a two-proportion z-test (or chi-square) for binary conversion metrics. Use a Welch t-test only for continuous metrics (revenue, time-on-page). Never use a t-test for binary outcome data.
rule.rationale: A t-test on binary (0/1) data violates the normality assumption for small samples and produces inflated false-positive rates. The z-test for proportions is exact for binomial outcomes.

rule.statement: Report effect size and confidence interval, not just p-value. Use Wilson score CI for proportions; bootstrap CI for long-tailed metrics like revenue.
rule.rationale: p-value alone gives no information about the size of the effect. A 0.001% lift can be statistically significant with enough users — the CI shows whether the effect is practically meaningful.

rule.statement: Pre-compute the experiment end date from sample size. Refuse to read out results before the end date unless using a sequential test method (mSPRT, always-valid CIs).
rule.rationale: Peeking at p-values on day 1 and stopping on the first "significant" result inflates the false-positive rate from 5% to 20–30%. The pre-registered end date prevents this.

section.note: Sample-size code: from math import ceil; from statsmodels.stats.power import NormalIndPower. def sample_size_per_arm(p_baseline: float, mde_relative: float, alpha=0.05, power=0.8): p2 = p_baseline * (1 + mde_relative); effect = (p2 - p_baseline) / ((p_baseline * (1 - p_baseline)) ** 0.5); n = NormalIndPower().solve_power(effect_size=effect, alpha=alpha, power=power, ratio=1.0); return ceil(n). For baseline 5% conversion, want to detect +10% relative lift: sample_size_per_arm(0.05, 0.10).

section.name: antipatterns
section.p: Common A/B testing failures: random assignment, missing exposures, peeking, wrong statistical test.

antipattern.reason: Produces a different variant on every page load, request, or process restart. The same user alternates between variants, cross-contaminating both groups and making results meaningless.

antipattern.description: Running successive experiments on the same population without changing the hash salt (experiment ID).
antipattern.reason: Correlated assignment: users in control for experiment 1 are always in control for experiment 2. This produces correlated bias that looks like real effects.

antipattern.description: Stopping the experiment the moment the p-value crosses 0.05 (peeking).
antipattern.reason: Multiple comparisons over time inflate the false-positive rate. Stopping on first significance yields a 20–30% false-positive rate instead of the intended 5%.

antipattern.description: Using `scipy.stats.ttest_ind` on a binary (0/1) conversion metric.
antipattern.reason: The t-test assumes approximately normal data. Binary metrics violate this for small or imbalanced samples, producing incorrect p-values. Use chi-square or two-proportion z-test.

antipattern.description: Logging conversions without logging exposures, then computing conversion rate as conversions / total_users.
antipattern.reason: Users not exposed to the experiment are included in the denominator, diluting the conversion rate and obscuring real effects. Denominator must be unique exposed users per variant.

section.name: agentic-workflow-and-best-practices
section.p: Treat experimentation as a contract: agent reads experiment.yaml (id, variants, weights, eligibility, primary metric, MDE) and generates: (1) typed assigner with deterministic hash, (2) exposure + conversion event schema, (3) SQL or stats notebook for the readout. Separate agent runs SRM check before any p-value: if chi2(observed_split, expected_split) > threshold, abort. A reviewer agent re-derives sample-size and refuses to "call" results before the precomputed end date unless using a sequential-test method (mSPRT, always-valid CIs).
Pre-register hypothesis, primary metric, MDE, sample size, and stop date. Treat any deviation as a new experiment.
Deterministic hashing only: hash(user_id || experiment_id) mod 100. Never random.choice — breaks stickiness across processes.
Salt per experiment so that successive experiments do not share buckets (correlated bias).
Log exposure on every render, dedupe on (exp_id, user_id, day). Without exposure log you cannot filter to "actually saw it".
Daily SRM check: chi-square exposure counts vs expected weights; pause experiment if p < 0.001.
Report effect size + CI, not just p-value. Use Wilson CI for proportions, bootstrap for revenue per user (long-tailed).
One primary metric, ≤3 secondaries, all guardrails (latency, error rate, churn) tracked but not stop-gated unless degraded.
Run for full business cycles (≥1 week, often 2) to absorb day-of-week effects and novelty.
LLMs reach for random.choice([A, B]) — destroys stickiness and reproducibility. Force a deterministic hash with hashlib.sha256 mod weight buckets.
Agents log conversions but forget exposures; readout becomes "users who converted while assigned to X" without denominator. Demand both events.
Sample-size code generated by LLMs frequently confuses absolute and relative MDE; require explicit mde_relative=0.05 # 5% lift over baseline in the contract.
Agents will compute p-value with scipy.stats.ttest_ind on a binary metric — wrong test. Use chi-square / two-proportion z-test for binomial, t-test only for continuous.
"Stop the test, it's significant" pattern: peeking. Bake the stop date into the assigner config and refuse readouts before then unless using a sequential-test method (mSPRT, always-valid CIs).
Bot/crawler traffic skews to the variant served on cold cache → SRM. Agent must filter is_bot before computing exposure ratios.
Human-in-loop checkpoint: launch decision and shipping decision must be human; agent only produces readouts and recommendations, never auto-promotes a variant.

section.name: tools-and-services
section.p: CLI tools and SaaS platforms for experimentation. GrowthBook CLI: Open-source experimentation platform; flag + analysis; npm i -g growthbook-cli · growthbook.io. Statsig CLI: Statsig flags, experiments, analytics; npm. Eppo CLI: Eppo experiment platform; npm. jupytext + papermill: Parametrised stats notebooks for readouts; pip install papermill. tea-tasting (Python): Pragmatic experiment statistics (Welch t-test, CI); pip install tea-tasting. stats-can / scipy.stats: Underlying statistical tests; pip install scipy.
GrowthBook: OSS + SaaS; Yes — REST + SDK + CLI; Self-host on Postgres/ClickHouse; agent can manage experiments via API.
Statsig: SaaS; Yes — REST + SDK; Strong CUPED + sequential testing.
Eppo: SaaS; Yes — Terraform + API; Warehouse-native (Snowflake/BigQuery), no event pipeline rebuild.
LaunchDarkly: SaaS; Yes — REST API; Flag-first; experimentation is a paid add-on.
PostHog: OSS + SaaS; Yes — REST API + SDK; Flags + product analytics + experiments in one.
Optimizely: SaaS; Partial; Heavier UI workflow; less agent-friendly.
Amplitude Experiment: SaaS; Yes; If you already use Amplitude analytics.
Segment + warehouse: SaaS; Yes; Useful as the event pipe feeding any of the above.

template.purpose: ExperimentAnalyzer with full statistical analysis and ExperimentResults dataclass.
Sample size calculator per arm using NormalIndPower.
